title: Reading Practice (Generated)
description: LLM-generated reading comprehension drills, one lesson per VOA Learning English passage.

lessons.title: 'Watching the Grass Grow' Is Not Fun

content.introduction: Read "'Watching the Grass Grow' Is Not Fun" and answer the questions.

prompt.passage: Springtime is a time of renewal and growth. Many plants end their long winter sleep and form new buds and shoots.
prompt.question: What happens to many plants during springtime?

grounded_on.title: 'Watching the Grass Grow' Is Not Fun

prompt.passage: That idea is what gives us the expression “like watching grass grow.” We use this expression to describe an experience that is uninteresting.
prompt.question: What does the expression “like watching grass grow” mean?

grounded_on.title: 'Watching the Grass Grow' Is Not Fun

prompt.passage: After the politician won his campaign, he gave a two-hour long acceptance speech. Two hours! It was as much fun as watching grass grow.
prompt.question: How long was the politician's speech?

grounded_on.title: 'Watching the Grass Grow' Is Not Fun

prompt.passage: For people who are always active, always moving, always on-the-go, we can say they don't let grass grow under their feet.
prompt.question: Which people are described by the expression ‘don’t let grass grow under your feet’?

grounded_on.title: 'Watching the Grass Grow' Is Not Fun

prompt.passage: Anna Matteo wrote this story for VOA Learning English.
prompt.question: Who wrote this story?

grounded_on.title: 'Watching the Grass Grow' Is Not Fun

lessons.title: Green Means Spring and Other Things

content.introduction: Read "Green Means Spring and Other Things" and answer the questions.

prompt.passage: Green is the color of grass and the leaves on trees.
prompt.question: What color is described as the color of grass and leaves on trees?

grounded_on.title: Green Means Spring and Other Things

prompt.passage: In the fifteenth century, a greenhorn was a young cow or ox whose horns had not yet developed.
prompt.question: What does the word 'greenhorn' originally mean in the fifteenth century?

grounded_on.title: Green Means Spring and Other Things

prompt.passage: Someone who has the ability to grow plants well is said to have a green thumb.
prompt.question: What does the expression 'green thumb' mean?
options: someone who is good at cooking
someone who can grow plants well
someone who is good at math
someone who is a new worker

answer_key.answer: someone who can grow plants well

grounded_on.title: Green Means Spring and Other Things

prompt.passage: In most places in the world, a green light is a sign to move ahead.
prompt.question: What does a green light on a traffic signal mean?

grounded_on.title: Green Means Spring and Other Things

prompt.passage: Marilyn Christiano wrote this program.
prompt.question: Who wrote the program 'Words and Their Stories'?

grounded_on.title: Green Means Spring and Other Things

content.introduction: Read "How to 'Dish Up' Something Good" and answer the questions.

prompt.passage: But, a signature dish is a dish we are known for making well. In a way, it identifies us.
prompt.question: What does a signature dish show about a person?
options: It shows what they are known for cooking.

answer_key.answer: It shows what they are known for cooking.

prompt.passage: When the rolls are on the table, people know that I made them.
prompt.question: Why do people know the speaker made the rolls?
options: Because the rolls have the speaker's name on them.
Because the rolls are on the table.
Because the speaker announced it loudly.
Because the rolls are very salty.

answer_key.answer: Because the rolls are on the table.

prompt.passage: To dish can mean to talk about private or personal information. It might be gossip.
prompt.question: What can "dish" mean?

prompt.passage: We can also dish out things, like jokes or even insults.
prompt.question: What does "dish out" mean in this context?

prompt.passage: We say, “They can dish it out, but they can’t take it.” They are able and willing to share harsh thoughts, criticisms, or insults about others, but they do not think they, themselves, deserve such treatment.
prompt.question: What does the phrase "they can dish it out, but they can't take it" mean?
options: They can give insults but cannot receive them.
They can eat food but cannot drink.
They can talk but cannot listen.
They can run but cannot walk.

answer_key.answer: They can give insults but cannot receive them.

title: Reading Practice 2 (Generated)
description: LLM-generated reading comprehension drills, second batch of VOA Learning English passages.

content.introduction: Read "Sometimes We 'Spread Ourself Too Thin'" and answer the questions.

prompt.passage: If we work on many projects simultaneously, or at the same time, it is possible that none will get done well, if they get done at all.
prompt.question: What can happen if we work on many projects at the same time?
options: All projects will be finished quickly.
No project will be finished well.
Projects will be done in order.

answer_key.answer: No project will be finished well.

prompt.passage: Being spread too thin can also be described as biting off more than we can chew.
prompt.question: What is another way to describe being spread too thin?
options: Biting off more than we can chew.

answer_key.answer: Biting off more than we can chew.

prompt.passage: Many people have family and work responsibilities. We might have religious or community activities.
prompt.question: Which of the following is mentioned as part of people's responsibilities?

prompt.passage: A: Sounds like they’ve spread themselves too thin. They’ve got too much going on.
prompt.question: What does the speaker think about the students?

prompt.passage: And then I’m going to warn them about biting off more than they can chew.
prompt.question: What will the teacher do?
options: Warn them about biting off more than they can chew.

answer_key.answer: Warn them about biting off more than they can chew.

lessons.title: Knee Jerk and Gut Reactions: When We Are Not in Control

content.introduction: Read "Knee Jerk and Gut Reactions: When We Are Not in Control" and answer the questions.

prompt.passage: Meriam Webster defines the noun 'knee jerk' as 'an involuntary forward kick produced by a light blow on the tendon below the kneecap.'
prompt.question: What is the definition of a knee jerk according to Meriam Webster?
options: A sudden decision made at work
An involuntary forward kick produced by a light blow on the tendon below the kneecap

answer_key.answer: An involuntary forward kick produced by a light blow on the tendon below the kneecap

grounded_on.title: Knee Jerk and Gut Reactions: When We Are Not in Control

prompt.passage: Now, a gut reaction also happens without thinking. But it is a bit different than a knee-jerk response. A gut reaction is a feeling. When we have a gut reaction, our instincts are telling us that something may not be right. Gut reactions are instinctual warnings.
prompt.question: How does a gut reaction differ from a knee-jerk reaction?
options: It is always physical, while knee-jerk is mental
It is a feeling that warns something may be wrong, while knee-jerk is automatic
It happens only in sports, while knee-jerk happens in everyday life
It is a conscious decision, while knee-jerk is unconscious

answer_key.answer: It is a feeling that warns something may be wrong, while knee-jerk is automatic

grounded_on.title: Knee Jerk and Gut Reactions: When We Are Not in Control

prompt.passage: We often use 'knee jerk' as an adjective.
prompt.question: In the passage, 'knee jerk' is most often used as a:

grounded_on.title: Knee Jerk and Gut Reactions: When We Are Not in Control

prompt.passage: If brought to account, the player always blames the dropped ball on other teammates. You can say that player is having a knee-jerk reaction to criticism.
prompt.question: What did the player do when criticized?
options: He took responsibility for the mistake

grounded_on.title: Knee Jerk and Gut Reactions: When We Are Not in Control

prompt.passage: I later found out that a dangerous bear had been sighted in the same area that I had fled. I am so thankful I listened to my gut instinct.
prompt.question: Why did the narrator decide to turn around and go down the mountain?
options: Because the view was too far away
Because a dangerous bear had been sighted nearby

answer_key.answer: Because a dangerous bear had been sighted nearby

grounded_on.title: Knee Jerk and Gut Reactions: When We Are Not in Control

content.introduction: Read "The Story of Jack Frost" and answer the questions.

prompt.passage: Jack Frost represents the coming of dark, cold days. He is the personification of winter, a human form of winter weather.
prompt.question: What does Jack Frost represent?

prompt.passage: When temperatures drop and a cold snap comes, that means one thing: Jack Frost has arrived!
prompt.question: When a cold snap comes, what happens?

prompt.passage: When a frost or deep freeze comes, you had better bundle up! You should wear enough clothing to stay warm.
prompt.question: What should you do when frost comes?

prompt.passage: That is what poet Hannah Flagg Gould (1789-1865) must have been thinking when she wrote a poem called The Frost.
prompt.question: Who wrote the poem "The Frost"?

prompt.passage: If you’re wearing that thin coat…you’ll catch your death!!
prompt.question: According to the conversation, what might happen if you don’t wear a warm coat?

title: Reading Practice 3 (Generated)
description: LLM-generated reading comprehension drills, third batch of VOA Learning English passages.

content.introduction: Read "'Kicking Off' a New Year" and answer the questions.

prompt.passage: One of the most popular sports in the United States is American football. In the U.S., “football” is a sport that involves a lot of contact.
prompt.question: Which sport is described as involving a lot of contact in the U.S.?

prompt.passage: The term kickoff is commonly used to describe the start of an event or project. For example, we could say experts from all over the world attended the kickoff of the new language museum. You can use it as a phrasal verb, as well. For example, people often like to “kick off” the new year with a resolution, or promise to yourself, to reach a goal or to do something.
prompt.question: What does the phrase "kick off" mean in American English?
options: To start an event or project

answer_key.answer: To start an event or project

prompt.passage: Football also has the term “punt.” Punting is another sort of kick a team uses when they cannot score. The punter kicks a ball with the top of his foot. “Punt,” like “kick,” can be both a noun and a verb.
prompt.question: What is a "punt" in football?

prompt.passage: If you say that someone has ‘moved the goalposts,’ you are accusing them of changing the rules or end goals to make it difficult for you to succeed.
prompt.question: What does "moving the goalposts" mean?
options: Changing the rules to make it harder

answer_key.answer: Changing the rules to make it harder

prompt.passage: To do this, the kicker must kick the football between the goalposts at either end of the football field. If he succeeds, his team gets three points.
prompt.question: How many points are awarded for a successful field goal?

content.introduction: Read "Expressions for New Year's Resolution" and answer the questions.

prompt.passage: "The word “venture” can be both a noun and a verb. As a verb, it means to proceed in a dangerous or risky plan. As a noun, it means an action that involves chance, risk, or danger."
prompt.question: According to the passage, what does the noun 'venture' mean?
options: an action that involves chance, risk, or danger
to proceed in a dangerous plan

answer_key.answer: an action that involves chance, risk, or danger

prompt.passage: "Fortune" usually means wealth or fame. But it can also mean being in a good situation. And, if you are bold, you are brave. So, this expression means that people who are brave and take chances help themselves.
prompt.question: What does the expression 'fortune favors the bold' suggest?
options: Brave people who take chances help themselves
Bold people never get bad outcomes

answer_key.answer: Brave people who take chances help themselves

prompt.passage: "Such a change could involve moving to a new city, starting a new job, or ending a relationship."
prompt.question: Which of the following is mentioned as a risky change?

prompt.passage: "A friend of mine wanted to learn how to drive a stunt car and then move to Los Angeles to become a stunt car driver."
prompt.question: What did the friend want to become after learning to drive a stunt car?

prompt.passage: "This means that without doing something risky or difficult, a bad situation will not improve. You could also sum it up this way: If you try something risky, you could fail. But if you do not try at all … you will surely fail."
prompt.question: According to the passage, what happens if you don't try something risky?

content.introduction: Read "An Eye for an Eye" and answer the questions.

prompt.passage: The expression an eye for an eye, a tooth for a tooth is ancient. It appears in The Bible and in The Code of Hammurabi, a set of laws written by the ancient Babylonian king of that name.
prompt.question: In which texts does the expression appear?
options: The Bible and in The Code of Hammurabi
The Bible and in the Old Testament
The Bible and in the New Testament
The Bible and in The Code of Hammurabi and the Quran

answer_key.answer: The Bible and in The Code of Hammurabi

prompt.passage: In kind means “in the same way.”
prompt.question: What does 'in kind' mean?

prompt.passage: Tit for tat literally means blow for blow.
prompt.question: What does 'tit for tat' literally mean?

prompt.passage: Some people wait years or a lifetime to get their revenge.
prompt.question: How long might some people wait for revenge?

prompt.passage: Revenge is a dish best served cold. The idea is that settling the score over time without emotion is the best way.
prompt.question: Which expression says revenge should be served cold?
options: Revenge is a dish best served hot
Revenge is a dish best served cold
Revenge is a dish best served early
Revenge is a dish best served later

answer_key.answer: Revenge is a dish best served cold

title: Reading Practice — A1 (Generated)

content.introduction: Read "Dialogue 1-1: Formal Greetings" and answer the questions.

prompt.passage:
Dialogue 1-1: Formal Greetings
JAMES: Good morning, Professor Austin, how are you doing?
PROFESSOR AUSTIN: Good morning, James. I am doing well. And you?
JAMES: I’m great, thank you. This is my friend Emma. She is thinking about applying to this college. She has a few questions. Would you mind telling us about the process, please?
PROFESSOR AUSTIN: Hello, Emma! It’s a pleasure to meet you. I’m more than happy to speak with you. Please stop by my office next week.
EMMA: I t’s a pleasure to meet you, professor. Thank you so much for helping us.
PROFESSOR AUSTIN: Don’t mention it. Hopefully, I will be able to answer your questions!
prompt.question: Who says "Good morning, Professor Austin, how are you doing?"

content.introduction: Read "Dialogue 1-2: Informal Greetings and Farewells" and answer the questions.

prompt.passage:
Dialogue 1-2: Informal Greetings and Farewells
JANE: Hi, Helen! How’s it going?
HELEN: Fine, thanks — and you?
JANE: Just fine. Where are you off to?
HELEN: To the library. I’ve got a history exam next week and need to start studying. Ugh.
JANE: Oh, no. Well, I’ll see you later then. Good luck!
HELEN: Thanks. See you later.
prompt.question: Who is Jane talking to?

title: Reading Practice — A1, Part 2 (Generated)

content.introduction: Read "Dialogue 1-4: Informal Introductions" and answer the questions.

prompt.passage: JIM: Who’s the tall woman next to Barbara?
prompt.question: Who is the tall woman next to Barbara?

prompt.passage: JIM: No, I wasn’t at Steve’s party.
prompt.question: Did Jim attend Steve’s party?

prompt.passage: CHARLES: O h! Then let me introduce you to her now. Mary, this is my friend Jim.
prompt.question: Who is Charles introducing to Jim?

prompt.passage: JIM: Would you like a drink?
prompt.question: What does Jim ask Mary?

prompt.passage: MARY: Sure, let’s go get one.

content.introduction: Read "Dialogue 1.5: What Time Is It?" and answer the questions.

prompt.passage:
NATASHA: What time is it? We’re going to be late!
TONY: It’s a quarter after seven. We’re on time. Don’t panic.
prompt.question: What time does Tony say it is?

prompt.passage: TONY: Sure we will. Rush hour is almost over. Anyway, the party starts at 8:00.
prompt.question: When does the party start?

prompt.passage: NATASHA: But I thought we had to be at the restaurant by 7:30 for the surprise party. We’ll never make it there with all this evening traffic.
prompt.question: Why does Natasha think they will be late?

prompt.passage: TONY: Sure we will. Rush hour is almost over. Anyway, the party starts at 8:00. But I do need help with directions. Can you call the restaurant and ask them where we park our car?
prompt.question: Who is asked to call the restaurant?

prompt.passage: TONY: Sure we will. Rush hour is almost over. Anyway, the party starts at 8:00.
prompt.question: What does Tony say about rush hour?

content.introduction: Read "Dialogue 1-6: A Telephone Call" and answer the questions.

prompt.passage: JOHN: Hi, Alice, it’s John. How are you?
prompt.question: Who is speaking in this line?

prompt.passage: ALICE: Oh, hi, John! I was just thinking about you.
prompt.question: Who is responding to John?

prompt.passage: JOHN: That’s nice. I was wondering if you’d like to go to a movie tonight.
prompt.question: What does John want to do with Alice?

prompt.passage: JOHN: I was thinking about that new comedy Lights Out. What do you think?
prompt.question: What is the name of the movie?

prompt.passage: ALICE: Sure, I’d love to! What’s playing?
prompt.question: What time will John pick Alice up?

title: Reading Practice — A1, Part 3 (Generated)
description: Third batch of A1 reading-comprehension drills from State Dept Everyday Conversations dialogues.

lessons.title: Dialogue 1-7: Can You Say That Again?

content.introduction: Read "Dialogue 1-7: Can You Say That Again?" and answer the questions.

prompt.passage:
Luke: Hello? Hi, Stephanie, how are things at the office?
Stephanie: H i, Luke! How are you? Can you please stop and pick up extra paper for the computer printer?
Luke: What did you say? Can you repeat that, please? Did you say to pick up ink for the printer? Sorry, the phone is cutting out.
Stephanie: Can you hear me now? No, I need more computer paper. Listen, I’ll text you exactly what I need. Thanks, Luke. Talk to you later.
Luke: Thanks, Stephanie. Sorry, my phone has really bad reception here.
prompt.question: Who is speaking at the beginning of the conversation?

grounded_on.title: Dialogue 1-7: Can You Say That Again?

content.introduction: Read "Dialogue 1-8: Coincidences" and answer the questions.

prompt.passage: MEG: Well, hello there, Julia! Long time no see! JULIA: Meg! Hi! What a coincidence! I haven’t seen you in ages! What are you doing here?

prompt.passage: MEG: I just got a new job in the city, so I’m shopping for some clothes. Hey, what do you think of this shirt? JULIA: Hmmm … well, you know how much I love blue. See? I’ve got the same shirt!
prompt.question: What does Julia say about the shirt?

prompt.question: What did Meg just get?

prompt.question: What do Meg and Julia have in common?

content.introduction: Read "Dialogue 1-9: Weather Report" and answer the questions.

prompt.passage:
JENNIFER: It’s freezing outside! What happened to the weather report? I thought this cold front was supposed to pass.
GABRIELA: Yeah, I thought so too. That’s what I read online this morning.
JENNIFER: I guess the wind chill is really driving down the temperature.
GABRIELA: Can we go inside? I feel like my toes are starting to go numb.
prompt.question: What does JENNIFER think is causing the temperature to drop?

prompt.passage:
JENNIFER: It’s freezing outside! What happened to the weather report? I thought this cold front was supposed to pass.
GABRIELA: Yeah, I thought so too. That’s what I read online this morning.
JENNIFER: I guess the wind chill is really driving down the temperature.
GABRIELA: Can we go inside? I feel like my toes are starting to go numb.
prompt.question: Where does GABRIELA want to go?

title: Reading Practice — B2 (Generated)
description: LLM-generated reading-comprehension drills based on O. Henry short stories (B2 literary prose).

content.introduction: Read "The Gift of the Magi" and answer the questions.

prompt.passage: "One dollar and eighty-seven cents. That was all."
prompt.question: How much money did Della have to buy a present for Jim?

prompt.passage: "One was Jim’s gold watch that had been his father’s and his grandfather’s."
prompt.question: Who originally owned Jim’s gold watch?

prompt.passage: "Where she stopped the sign read: “Mme. Sofronie. Hair Goods of All Kinds.”"
prompt.question: What was the name of the shop where Della bought her hair?
options: Mme. Sofronie. Hair Goods of All Kinds

answer_key.answer: Mme. Sofronie. Hair Goods of All Kinds

prompt.passage: "He simply stared at her fixedly with that peculiar expression on his face."
prompt.question: How did Jim react when he saw Della with her hair cut?
options: "He simply stared at her fixedly with that peculiar expression on his face."

answer_key.answer: "He simply stared at her fixedly with that peculiar expression on his face."

prompt.passage: "I sold the watch to get the money to buy your combs."
prompt.question: What did Jim sell to buy Della’s combs?
options: "I sold the watch to get the money to buy your combs."
"I sold the fob chain to get the money to buy your combs."
"I sold the overcoat to get the money to buy your combs."
"I sold the hair to get the money to buy your combs."

answer_key.answer: "I sold the watch to get the money to buy your combs."

content.introduction: Read "A Cosmopolite in a Café" and answer the questions.

prompt.passage: "I pay rent on a room in Cairo, Egypt, and another in Yokohama all the year around."
prompt.question: Which city does the cosmopolite mention as a place where he pays rent?

prompt.passage: "I know the South, and when the band plays ‘Dixie’ I like to observe. I have formed the belief that the man who applauds that air with special violence and ostensible sectional loyalty is invariably a native of either Secaucus, N.J., or the district between Murray Hill Lyceum and the Harlem River, this city."
prompt.question: According to the narrator, people who applaud “Dixie” with special violence are most likely from which area?
options: The district between Murray Hill Lyceum and the Harlem River
New York City’s Lower East Side

prompt.passage: "A relic of the stone age," declared Coglan, warmly.
prompt.question: How does the cosmopolite feel about patriotism?
options: He decries it as a relic of the stone age.
He embraces it as a modern value.

answer_key.answer: He decries it as a relic of the stone age.

prompt.passage: "The man with the red tie" (that was my cosmopolite), said he, "got hot on account of things said about the bum sidewalks and water supply of the place he come from by the other guy."
prompt.question: According to McCarthy, why did the cosmopolite become upset?
options: Because of remarks about sidewalks and water supply.
Because he was from Mattawamkeag, Maine.
Because he had a red tie.

answer_key.answer: Because of remarks about sidewalks and water supply.

prompt.passage: "At midnight the café was crowded. By some chance the little table at which I sat had escaped the eye of incomers, and two vacant chairs at it extended their arms with venal hospitality to the influx of patrons."
prompt.question: How would you best describe the café’s atmosphere?

content.introduction: Read "The Cop and the Anthem" and answer the questions.

prompt.passage: Three months on the Island was what his soul craved. Three months of assured board and bed and congenial company, safe from Boreas and bluecoats, seemed to Soapy the essence of things desirable.
prompt.question: What was the main reason Soapy wanted to go to the Island?

prompt.passage: The pleasantest was to dine luxuriously at some expensive restaurant; and then, after declaring insolvency, be handed over quietly and without uproar to a policeman.
prompt.question: How did Soapy first try to get into the expensive restaurant?
options: By pretending to be a wealthy patron
By declaring insolvency and asking a policeman

answer_key.answer: By declaring insolvency and asking a policeman

prompt.passage: The conjunction of Soapy’s receptive state of mind and the influences about the old church wrought a sudden and wonderful change in his soul. The organist played cemented Soapy to the iron fence, for he had known it well in the days when his life contained such things as mothers and roses and ambitions and friends and immaculate thoughts and collars.
prompt.question: What event caused Soapy to have a sudden change of heart?
options: Seeing a policeman on the street
Hearing organ music at a church

answer_key.answer: Hearing organ music at a church

prompt.passage: Soapy scorned the provisions made in the name of charity for the city’s dependents. In Soapy’s opinion the Law was more benign than Philanthropy. There was an endless round of institutions, municipal and eleemosynary, on which he might set out and receive lodging and food accordant with the simple life. But to one of Soapy’s proud spirit the gifts of charity are encumbered. If not in coin you must pay in humiliation of spirit for every benefit received at the hands of philanthropy.
prompt.question: What is the main reason Soapy despises charity?
options: It forces him to give up his dignity
He hates the people who run charities

answer_key.answer: It forces him to give up his dignity

prompt.passage: Soapy stood still, with his hands in his pockets, and smiled at the sight of brass buttons. "Where’s the man that done that?" inquired the officer excitedly. "Don’t you figure out that I might have had something to do with it?" said Soapy, not without sarcasm, but friendly, as one greets good fortune.
prompt.question: Which of the following best describes Soapy’s plan after breaking into the shop window?

prompt.passage: True!—nervous—very, very dreadfully nervous I had been and am; but why will you say that I am mad?
prompt.question: Which word completes the excerpt: True!—nervous—______, very dreadfully nervous I had been and am; but why will you say that I am mad?

prompt.passage: The disease had sharpened my senses—not destroyed—not dulled them.
prompt.question: Which word completes the excerpt: The disease had sharpened my ______—not destroyed—not dulled them.

prompt.passage: I heard all things in the heaven and in the earth.
prompt.question: Which word completes the excerpt: I heard all things in the ______ and in the earth.

prompt.passage: and observe how healthily—how calmly I can tell you the whole story.
prompt.question: Which word completes the excerpt: and observe how healthily—how ______ I can tell you the whole story.

prompt.passage: It is impossible to say how first the idea entered my brain; but once conceived, it haunted me day and night.

content.introduction: Read "The Black Cat" and answer the questions.

prompt.passage: For the most wild, yet most homely narrative which I am about to pen, I neither expect nor solicit belief.
prompt.question: Which word completes the excerpt: For the ______ wild, yet most homely narrative which I am about to pen, I neither expect nor solicit belief.

prompt.passage: Mad indeed would I be to expect it, in a case where my very senses reject their own evidence.
prompt.question: Which word completes the excerpt: Mad indeed would I be to ______ it, in a case where my very senses reject their own evidence.

prompt.passage: Yet, mad am I not—and very surely do I not dream.
prompt.question: Which word completes the excerpt: Yet, mad am I not—and very surely do I not ______.

prompt.passage: But to-morrow I die, and to-day I would unburthen my soul.
prompt.question: Which word completes the excerpt: But to-morrow I die, and to-day I ______ unburthen my soul.

prompt.passage: My immediate purpose is to place before the world, plainly, succinctly, and without comment, a series of mere household events.
prompt.question: Which word completes the excerpt: My immediate purpose is to ______ before the world, plainly, succinctly, and without comment, a series of mere household events.

content.introduction: Read "The Cask of Amontillado" and answer the questions.

prompt.passage: I have my doubts… I paid the full Amontillado price without consulting you in the matter.
prompt.question: Who paid the full price for the Amontillado?

prompt.passage: The vaults are insufferably damp. They are encrusted with nitre.
prompt.question: What did the narrator say was the main reason to go to the vaults?

prompt.passage: It was now midnight, and my task was drawing to a close. I had completed the eighth, the ninth, and the tenth tier. I had finished a portion of the last and the eleventh; there remained but a single stone to be fitted and plastered in.
prompt.question: How many tiers had the narrator completed before fitting the final stone?

prompt.passage: From one of these depended a short chain, from the other a padlock.
prompt.question: Which item did the narrator use to bind Fortunato to the wall?

prompt.passage: We had passed through a range of low arches, descended, passed on, and descending again, arrived at a deep crypt, in which the foulness of the air caused our flambeaux rather to glow than flame.
prompt.question: What did the narrator use to light the crypt?
